sentiment.rationale: An intimate opening of mutual admiration and longing between the beloved and her lover, evoking tender devotion and beauty.
Celebrates the joy of love with vivid imagery of springtime, the beloved's beauty, and the delight of companionship.
The beloved searches anxiously for her lover at night, finding him and refusing to let go, culminating in Solomon's royal procession.
An extended praise of the beloved's beauty, likened to a garden enclosed and a fountain sealed—imagery of purity and devotion.
The beloved misses her lover's knock and searches the city for him, suffering mistreatment, yet passionately describes his beauty.
The lover is found and praised with royal imagery; the beloved is described as uniquely beautiful and awe-inspiring.
Continued admiration of the beloved's form and beauty, with expressions of mutual desire and the sweetness of love.
Concludes with declarations that love is as strong as death, many waters cannot quench it, and a final invitation to the beloved.

summaries.summary: The beloved and her lover express mutual admiration; she describes herself as dark but comely and asks where he feeds his flock.
The beloved is compared to a lily among thorns and the lover to an apple tree; he calls her to come away as winter has passed and spring has come.
The beloved searches for her lover by night through the city streets, finds him, and a description of Solomon's royal procession and wedding day follows.
The lover praises every feature of the beloved's beauty in extended metaphor, calling her a garden enclosed and a fountain sealed.
The beloved delays opening to her lover and he departs; she searches the city and is mistreated by the watchmen, then describes his appearance to the daughters of Jerusalem.
The daughters of Jerusalem ask where the beloved's lover has gone; he is found in his garden, and he praises her as beautiful, terrible as an army with banners.
The lover describes the beloved's beauty from feet to head, expressing desire; she invites him to go forth into the fields and villages together.
The beloved wishes for open affection, declares that love is as strong as death and many waters cannot quench it, and the poem concludes with a final exchange.

crossReferences.note: The image of the rose and the lily echoes Isaiah's vision of the desert blossoming.
The banner of love over the beloved parallels Christ's love for the church.
The garden enclosed and fountain sealed parallel the imagery of marital fidelity in Proverbs.
Love as strong as death and unquenchable by many waters parallels the enduring nature of charity.
Nothing can overcome love, echoing Paul's declaration that nothing can separate us from the love of God.

verses.reason: The opening plea for the kisses of his mouth—sets the tone for the entire poem.
'I am the rose of Sharon, and the lily of the valleys'—one of the most quoted verses in the book.
'His banner over me was love'—a widely memorized declaration of divine affection.
Finding the beloved and refusing to let go—an image of devoted seeking and holding fast.
'Thou art all fair, my love; there is no spot in thee'—a declaration of complete beauty and acceptance.
'He is altogether lovely'—a comprehensive declaration of the beloved's perfection.
'I am my beloved's, and my beloved is mine'—the quintessential statement of mutual belonging.
'Love is strong as death … many waters cannot quench love'—the most theologically significant verse in the book.
'If a man would give all the substance of his house for love, it would utterly be contemned'—love cannot be purchased.
